item.title: State-owned AI
item.description: Plus sideways CPI

item.title: ‘South Korea’s Amazon’ hit with record fine over data breach
item.description: Coupang told to pay $409mn after hack that exposed personal information of nearly two-thirds of population

item.title: Private equity parties as LPs wait for cash
item.description: The industry’s annual jamboree comes as firms struggle to sell holdings at desired valuations

item.title: Kathy Ruemmler helps search for her own replacement as Goldman’s top lawyer
item.description: Bank’s decision to back executive after she resigned from the role over ties to Jeffrey Epstein sparked internal divisions

item.title: Toyota and JLR warn ‘Made in Europe’ plan threatens investment and jobs
item.description: Proposals spark outcry from carmakers that export vehicles to EU from countries such as the UK, Turkey and Morocco

item.title: Can Europe build a new fighter jet after Germany scraps joint venture?
item.description: Collapse of €100bn project with France leaves countries at a crossroads

item.title: Why the much-feared crunch in oil markets has yet to arrive
item.description: Still-ample stocks and some more reactive producers and consumers look to have left the world better equipped to cope with disruption

item.title: A shorter third runway at Heathrow is better than none
item.description: The UK airport and its airlines should stop fighting and compromise on expansion

item.title: ‘We’re back in the Stone Age’: Russia’s spies turn off the internet
item.description: Shutdowns have made one of the world’s most online nations resort to cash, paper maps and pet cams

item.title: Emirates to offer insurance to tempt passengers back to Dubai
item.description: Airline will fly people home if they are stranded, says carrier’s president Tim Clark

item.title: Prediction market wagers on World Cup winner climb to $2bn
item.description: Bets on which country will triumph could become Polymarket’s biggest prediction market in history

item.title: Rightwing MPs increase use of Musk’s X as centre-left rivals quit
item.description: Majority of Labour and Liberal Democrat politicians are on alternative social media platform Bluesky, report shows

item.title: European funds split on buying SpaceX over governance concerns
item.description: Continent’s sustainability disclosure rules may limit access of funds managing €6.8tn to blockbuster IPO

item.title: Spain’s assets in US targeted by international investors during World Cup
item.description: Blasket Renewable Investments secures rights to pursue funds over dispute linked to ending of renewable energy subsidies

item.title: Iran war tightens ‘super-squeeze’ in metals markets
item.description: Copper and aluminium had been rallying before Middle East conflict hit supply

item.title: Can Marks and Spencer really be a go-to fashion destination?
item.description: Britain’s favourite retailer had a reputation for mid-life mum basics, not trends — but an image makeover seems to be under way

item.title: Too much inheritance tax planning is driving families apart
item.description: The not so great consequences of the UK’s £5.5tn wealth transfer

item.title: How SpaceX could pull millions of savers into Musk’s orbit
item.description: The world’s richest man is shooting for Mars. Ordinary people are coming along for the ride

item.title: We will need a new tax code for the wealth AI creates
item.description: The question isn’t whether mass underemployment arrives but whether we have a policy framework ready when it does

item.title: Four in five UK boards discussing which decisions should be led by AI
item.description: Business experts worry governance processes are failing to keep pace with the technology

item.title: US World Cup host cities face doubts over economic windfall
item.description: Organisers hope for a tourism boost, but economists warn projections may fall short

item.title: Curaçao’s World Cup leap turns into a tourism playbook
item.description: Corendon’s backing and Dick Advocaat’s management turn a debut on the world stage into an economic boost

item.title: Canada seeks to turn World Cup buzz into lasting growth
item.description: Officials say the tournament is driving unprecedented interest in football, even as doubts persist over the scale of any financial windfall

item.title: After the final whistle: media groups battle for World Cup attention
item.description: YouTube, podcasts and live events are opening new ways to monetise the tournament

item.title: AI and defence demand powers $82bn order book for Japan’s turbine titan
item.description: Analysts question whether Mitsubishi Heavy Industries is spending cash fast enough to secure future growth

item.title: Trump’s Iran war has propelled China’s cleantech industry
item.description: Disruption to global energy supplies sparks surge in demand for alternatives

item.title: Lockheed Martin cannot say when US allies will get Patriot missiles
item.description: Top executive says arms maker does not control allocation of PAC-3 interceptors, despite tripling production

item.title: FirstFT: Seven people charged over deadly Hong Kong fire
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: US launches fresh wave of strikes on Iran, and BoJ governor Kazuo Ueda admitted to hospital

item.title: UK to launch CO₂ supply push as pubs prepare for bumper beer sales
item.description: Consultation comes as football fans look forward to start of World Cup and month of drinking

item.title: US tech stocks sink as volatility flares up on Wall Street
item.description: Latest bout of selling comes ahead of SpaceX’s historic market debut
